title: Ex Ranges
subtitle: Every Ex command takes a range. Learn to speak it fluently.
summary: Ranges precede Ex commands and tell them which lines to act on. They can be line numbers (5,10), marks ('a,'b), patterns (/foo/,/bar/), or shortcuts (%, .).

blocks.text: An *Ex range* tells the command **which lines** to operate on. It comes before the command name, separated by no space: `:5,10s/foo/bar/g` runs `:s` on lines 5 through 10.

rows: Current line to end of file
Start of file to current line
From mark a to mark b
Last visual selection (auto-inserted when you press `:` from Visual)
From next line matching /foo/ to next line matching /bar/
From previous line matching /foo/ to next /bar/

blocks.title: How Ranges Are Parsed
blocks.text:
Vim parses the range left-to-right, building two line numbers (start, end). Each piece can be:

  • An absolute line number (`5`, `$`, `42`)
  • A symbol (`.` = current, `'a` = mark a)
  • A search (`/foo/` or `?foo?`)
  • An offset added to any of the above (`.+5`, `'a-3`, `/foo/+1`)

The two halves are separated by `,` or `;`. The difference: with `;` the cursor *moves* to the first part's line before evaluating the second — useful when the second uses `/pattern/` and you want the search to start from the first match.

**Backward ranges (`:10,5`) error out** unless you confirm with the `?` prompt that comes up.
After a visual selection, pressing {key::} auto-inserts `:'<,'>`. Type your command after — `:'<,'>s/foo/bar/g` is generated for you.
